revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[en:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[fa:قلعه تاکی‌یاما (توکیو)]][[ja:滝山城]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[en:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[fa:قلعه تاکی‌یاما (توکیو)]][[ja:滝山城]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[en:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[fa:قلعه تاکی‌یاما (توکیو)]][[ja:滝山城]]
{{short description|Castle ruins in Hachiōji, Japan}} {{Infobox military structure | name = Takiyama Castle | nativename-a={{lang|ja|滝山城}} | partof = | location = {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji}}, [[:en:Tokyo Prefecture|Tokyo Prefecture]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | image =Bridge of Takiyama Castle.jpg | image_size = | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Takiyama Castle | caption =Reconstructed bridge between Honmaru and Nakanomaru | type = {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Hirayama-style castle}} | height = | ownership = Ōishi clan, {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} | controlledby = | condition = ruins | built = 1521 | builder = Ōishi clan | used = | materials = | demolished = 1576? | battles = | past_commanders = {{ill|Ōishi Sadahisa|en|Ōishi Sadahisa|WD=Q11438189|lt=Ōishi Sadahisa}}, {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru}} | garrison = | occupants = | events = Battle of Takiyama |coordinates ={{coord|35|42|7.06|N|139|19|41.85|E|region:JP-13|display=inline,title}} |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takiyama Castle'''|滝山城|Takiyama-jō}} was a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} located in what is now the Tani neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Hachiōji, Tokyo|Hachiōji, Tokyo]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.city.hachioji.tokyo.jp/kankobunka/002/005/p003412.html |title=滝山城|publisher=Hachiōji city official| language=Japanese | accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Its ruins have protected as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2007.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/138237|title=滝山城跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 August 2020}}</ref>
==Situation== Takiyama Castle is located on a long ridge south of the {{ill|Tama River|en|Tama River|WD=Q1063006|lt=Tama River}}, next to the confluence of the Tama River coming from the [[:en:Ome, Tokyo|Ome]] area with the {{ill|Aki River|en|Aki River|WD=Q23043420|lt=Aki River}} from the {{ill|Okutama, Tokyo|en|Okutama, Tokyo|WD=Q598626|lt=Okutama}} areas of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}. The castle site and surrounding area are within the borders of the {{ill|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|en|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|WD=Q7678414|lt=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park}}.
==History== During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, this portion of Musashi Province was ruled by the Ōishi clan, vassals of the Ogigayatsu-branch of the [[Uesugi clan]] and ''{{ill|Shugodai|en|Shugodai|WD=Q568360|lt=Shugodai}}'' of Musashi Province. They were one of the major military powers in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}}. Ōishi Sadashige constructed Takiyama Castle in 1521 to replace an earlier stronghold at Takatsuki, approximately one kilometer away.<ref name="Kojo">{{cite web|url=https://www.travel.co.jp/guide/article/34505/ |title=滝山城|publisher=line Travel|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> However, the Uesugi clan was soon eclipsed by the rising power of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} based at {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara}}, who had conquered all of neighboring {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province}} by around 1530. The Hōjō then set their sights north, decisively defeating the Uesugi at the 1546 {{ill|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|en|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1064720|lt=Siege of Kawagoe Castle}}. The Ōishi clan was forced to submit to {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru}}, who made Takiyama Castle his field headquarters for the conquest of Musashi.
Ujiteru expanded and improved the defences of the castle.<ref name="Nihon">{{cite web|url=https://www.jp-history.info/castle/558.html |title=滝山城|date=10 May 2016 |publisher=日本の歴史ガイド|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref><ref name="Yomigaeru">{{cite web|url= http://takiyamajo.com/html/haikei.php |title=滝山城|publisher=よみがえる滝山城|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Prior to Hōjō Ujiteru, Takiyama Castle was a simple structure with two {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=enclosures}}, the Honmaru (central bailey) or Nakanomaru (intermediate bailey) with rudimentary defenses. Ujiteru added a Ninomaru (second bailey), Sannomaru (third bailey) and Komiya Kuruwa (Komiya enclosure), each surrounded by dry moats and earthen ramparts. Each gate was fortified with a {{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=''yagura''}} tower and with an [[kuruwa#umadashi|''umadashi'']] buffer area in front of the gate to make a direct front assault impossible.
In 1569, {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}}, the warlord of {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}} attempted to seize the castle during the Siege of Todoriko. The Takeda army had 20,000 men led by [[Takeda Katsuyori]] and seized the opposite bank of the Tama River. However, the true purpose of the attack was feint in an attempt to draw off Hōjō forces from Shingen's real target, which was Odawara Castle. Although Shingen managed to encircle and threaten Odawara for a week, he eventually gave up and returned to Kai Province, and the forces at Takiyama Castle were also withdrawn.<ref name="Nihon"/> One result of the battle was that it revealed to Hōjō Ujiteru critical weaknesses of Takiyama Castle. Not only were the defenses of the castle obsolete due to advances in military technology (such as the introduction of gunpowder), but the site was limited geographically and the castle area could not be expanded to allow for more defense in depth. Furthermore, the location of the castle proved that it was not an obstruction for Takeda army to attack the Hōjō home territory at Odawara. Therefore, a few years after the battle, Hōjō Ujiteru relocated to {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle}}, and by 1584 Takiyama Castle was abandoned.<ref name="Yomigaeru"/>
==Current== The castle is now only ruins, just some remnants of moats and earthworks. A bridge between Honmaru base and Nakanomaru base was reconstructed.<ref name="Kojo"/> The castle was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.<ref>{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}}</ref> It is located approximately ten minutes from "Takiyama Jyoshi Shita" bus stop of Nishi-Tokyo bus from the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hachiōji Station|en|Hachiōji Station|WD=Q800868|lt=Hachiōji Station}}.
==Gallery== <gallery> 滝山城の空堀.jpg|Dry Moat of Takiyama Castle View from Nakanomaru Compound.jpg|View from Nakanomaru Compound Gate of Honmaru.jpg|Masugata gate of Honmaru Compound Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle Moat of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Dry moat of Komiya Compound </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)]]
== Literature == *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles 1540-1640 | url=https://archive.org/details/ospreyfortressja00ospr | url-access=limited | last=Turnbull| first=Stephen| year=2003| publisher=Osprey Publishing| isbn=1-84176-429-9 | page= [https://archive.org/details/ospreyfortressja00ospr/page/n62 64] pages}}
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}}]]

The castle site and surrounding area are within the borders of the {{ill|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|en|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|WD=Q7678414|lt=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park}}.
==History== During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, this portion of Musashi Province was ruled by the Ōishi clan, vassals of the Ogigayatsu-branch of the [[Uesugi clan]] and ''{{ill|Shugodai|en|Shugodai|WD=Q568360|lt=Shugodai}}'' of Musashi Province. {{short description|Castle ruins in Hachiōji, Japan}} {{Infobox military structure | name = Takiyama Castle | nativename-a={{lang|ja|滝山城}} | partof = | location = {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji|1=Hachiōji}}, [[:en:Tokyo Prefecture|Tokyo Prefecture]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | image =Bridge of Takiyama Castle.jpg | image_size = | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Takiyama Castle | caption =Reconstructed bridge between Honmaru and Nakanomaru | type = {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Hirayama-style castle|1=Japanese castle}} | height = | ownership = Ōishi clan, {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} | controlledby = | condition = ruins | built = 1521 | builder = Ōishi clan | used = | materials = | demolished = 1576? | battles = | past_commanders = {{ill|Ōishi Sadahisa|en|Ōishi Sadahisa|WD=Q11438189|lt=Ōishi Sadahisa|1=Ōishi Sadahisa}}, {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}} | garrison = | occupants = | events = Battle of Takiyama |coordinates ={{coord|35|42|7.06|N|139|19|41.85|E|region:JP-13|display=inline,title}} |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takiyama Castle'''|滝山城|Takiyama-jō}} was a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in what is now the Tani neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Hachiōji, Tokyo|Hachiōji, Tokyo]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.city.hachioji.tokyo.jp/kankobunka/002/005/p003412.html |title=滝山城|publisher=Hachiōji city official| language=Japanese | accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Its ruins have protected as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2007.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/138237|title=滝山城跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 August 2020}}</ref>
==Situation== Takiyama Castle is located on a long ridge south of the {{ill|Tama River|en|Tama River|WD=Q1063006|lt=Tama River|1=Tama River}}, next to the confluence of the Tama River coming from the [[:en:Ome, Tokyo|Ome]] area with the {{ill|Aki River|en|Aki River|WD=Q23043420|lt=Aki River|1=Aki River}} from the {{ill|Okutama, Tokyo|en|Okutama, Tokyo|WD=Q598626|lt=Okutama|1=Okutama, Tokyo}} areas of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}. The castle site and surrounding area are within the borders of the {{ill|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|en|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|WD=Q7678414|lt=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|1=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park}}.
==History== During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, this portion of Musashi Province was ruled by the Ōishi clan, vassals of the Ogigayatsu-branch of the [[Uesugi clan]] and ''{{ill|Shugodai|en|Shugodai|WD=Q568360|lt=Shugodai|1=Shugodai}}'' of Musashi Province. They were one of the major military powers in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}. Ōishi Sadashige constructed Takiyama Castle in 1521 to replace an earlier stronghold at Takatsuki, approximately one kilometer away.<ref name="Kojo">{{cite web|url=https://www.travel.co.jp/guide/article/34505/ |title=滝山城|publisher=line Travel|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> However, the Uesugi clan was soon eclipsed by the rising power of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} based at {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara|1=Odawara Castle}}, who had conquered all of neighboring {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} by around 1530. The Hōjō then set their sights north, decisively defeating the Uesugi at the 1546 {{ill|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|en|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1064720|lt=Siege of Kawagoe Castle|1=Siege of Kawagoe Castle}}. The Ōishi clan was forced to submit to {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}}, who made Takiyama Castle his field headquarters for the conquest of Musashi.
Ujiteru expanded and improved the defences of the castle.<ref name="Nihon">{{cite web|url=https://www.jp-history.info/castle/558.html |title=滝山城|date=10 May 2016 |publisher=日本の歴史ガイド|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref><ref name="Yomigaeru">{{cite web|url= http://takiyamajo.com/html/haikei.php |title=滝山城|publisher=よみがえる滝山城|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Prior to Hōjō Ujiteru, Takiyama Castle was a simple structure with two {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=enclosures|1=Kuruwa}}, the Honmaru (central bailey) or Nakanomaru (intermediate bailey) with rudimentary defenses. Ujiteru added a Ninomaru (second bailey), Sannomaru (third bailey) and Komiya Kuruwa (Komiya enclosure), each surrounded by dry moats and earthen ramparts. Each gate was fortified with a {{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=''yagura''|1=Yagura (tower)}} tower and with an [[kuruwa#umadashi|''umadashi'']] buffer area in front of the gate to make a direct front assault impossible.
In 1569, {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen|1=Takeda Shingen}}, the warlord of {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} attempted to seize the castle during the Siege of Todoriko. The Takeda army had 20,000 men led by [[Takeda Katsuyori]] and seized the opposite bank of the Tama River. However, the true purpose of the attack was feint in an attempt to draw off Hōjō forces from Shingen's real target, which was Odawara Castle. Although Shingen managed to encircle and threaten Odawara for a week, he eventually gave up and returned to Kai Province, and the forces at Takiyama Castle were also withdrawn.<ref name="Nihon"/> One result of the battle was that it revealed to Hōjō Ujiteru critical weaknesses of Takiyama Castle. Not only were the defenses of the castle obsolete due to advances in military technology (such as the introduction of gunpowder), but the site was limited geographically and the castle area could not be expanded to allow for more defense in depth. Furthermore, the location of the castle proved that it was not an obstruction for Takeda army to attack the Hōjō home territory at Odawara. Therefore, a few years after the battle, Hōjō Ujiteru relocated to {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, and by 1584 Takiyama Castle was abandoned.<ref name="Yomigaeru"/>
==Current== The castle is now only ruins, just some remnants of moats and earthworks. A bridge between Honmaru base and Nakanomaru base was reconstructed.<ref name="Kojo"/> The castle was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.<ref>{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}}</ref> It is located approximately ten minutes from "Takiyama Jyoshi Shita" bus stop of Nishi-Tokyo bus from the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hachiōji Station|en|Hachiōji Station|WD=Q800868|lt=Hachiōji Station|1=Hachiōji Station}}.
==Gallery== <gallery> 滝山城の空堀.jpg|Dry Moat of Takiyama Castle View from Nakanomaru Compound.jpg|View from Nakanomaru Compound Gate of Honmaru.jpg|Masugata gate of Honmaru Compound Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle Moat of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Dry moat of Komiya Compound </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)]]
== Literature == *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles 1540-1640 | url=https://archive.org/details/ospreyfortressja00ospr | url-access=limited | last=Turnbull| first=Stephen| year=2003| publisher=Osprey Publishing| isbn=1-84176-429-9 | page= [https://archive.org/details/ospreyfortressja00ospr/page/n62 64] pages}}

{{short description|Castle ruins in Hachiōji, Japan}} {{Infobox military structure | name = Takiyama Castle | nativename-a={{lang|ja|滝山城}} | partof = | location = {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji|1=Hachiōji}}, [[:en:Tokyo Prefecture|Tokyo Prefecture]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | image =Bridge of Takiyama Castle.jpg | image_size = | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Takiyama Castle | caption =Reconstructed bridge between Honmaru and Nakanomaru | type = {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Hirayama-style castle|1=Japanese castle}} | height = | ownership = Ōishi clan, {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} | controlledby = | condition = ruins | built = 1521 | builder = Ōishi clan | used = | materials = | demolished = 1576? | battles = | past_commanders = {{ill|Ōishi Sadahisa|en|Ōishi Sadahisa|WD=Q11438189|lt=Ōishi Sadahisa|1=Ōishi Sadahisa}}, {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}} | garrison = | occupants = | events = Battle of Takiyama |coordinates ={{coord|35|42|7.06|N|139|19|41.85|E|region:JP-13|display=inline,title}} |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takiyama Castle'''|滝山城|Takiyama-jō}} was a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in what is now the Tani neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Hachiōji, Tokyo|Hachiōji, Tokyo]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.city.hachioji.tokyo.jp/kankobunka/002/005/p003412.html |title=滝山城|publisher=Hachiōji city official| language=Japanese | accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Its ruins have protected as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2007.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/138237|title=滝山城跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 August 2020}}</ref>
==Situation== Takiyama Castle is located on a long ridge south of the {{ill|Tama River|en|Tama River|WD=Q1063006|lt=Tama River|1=Tama River}}, next to the confluence of the Tama River coming from the [[:en:Ome, Tokyo|Ome]] area with the {{ill|Aki River|en|Aki River|WD=Q23043420|lt=Aki River|1=Aki River}} from the {{ill|Okutama, Tokyo|en|Okutama, Tokyo|WD=Q598626|lt=Okutama|1=Okutama, Tokyo}} areas of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}. The castle site and surrounding area are within the borders of the {{ill|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|en|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|WD=Q7678414|lt=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|1=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park}}.
==History== During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, this portion of Musashi Province was ruled by the Ōishi clan, vassals of the Ogigayatsu-branch of the [[Uesugi clan]] and ''{{ill|Shugodai|en|Shugodai|WD=Q568360|lt=Shugodai|1=Shugodai}}'' of Musashi Province. They were one of the major military powers in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}. Ōishi Sadashige constructed Takiyama Castle in 1521 to replace an earlier stronghold at Takatsuki, approximately one kilometer away.<ref name="Kojo">{{cite web|url=https://www.travel.co.jp/guide/article/34505/ |title=滝山城|publisher=line Travel|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> However, the Uesugi clan was soon eclipsed by the rising power of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} based at {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara|1=Odawara Castle}}, who had conquered all of neighboring {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} by around 1530. The Hōjō then set their sights north, decisively defeating the Uesugi at the 1546 {{ill|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|en|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1064720|lt=Siege of Kawagoe Castle|1=Siege of Kawagoe Castle}}. The Ōishi clan was forced to submit to {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}}, who made Takiyama Castle his field headquarters for the conquest of Musashi.
Ujiteru expanded and improved the defences of the castle.<ref name="Nihon">{{cite web|url=https://www.jp-history.info/castle/558.html |title=滝山城|date=10 May 2016 |publisher=日本の歴史ガイド|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref><ref name="Yomigaeru">{{cite web|url= http://takiyamajo.com/html/haikei.php |title=滝山城|publisher=よみがえる滝山城|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Prior to Hōjō Ujiteru, Takiyama Castle was a simple structure with two {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=enclosures|1=Kuruwa}}, the Honmaru (central bailey) or Nakanomaru (intermediate bailey) with rudimentary defenses. Ujiteru added a Ninomaru (second bailey), Sannomaru (third bailey) and Komiya Kuruwa (Komiya enclosure), each surrounded by dry moats and earthen ramparts. Each gate was fortified with a {{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=''yagura''|1=Yagura (tower)}} tower and with an [[kuruwa#umadashi|''umadashi'']] buffer area in front of the gate to make a direct front assault impossible.
In 1569, {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen|1=Takeda Shingen}}, the warlord of {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} attempted to seize the castle during the Siege of Todoriko. The Takeda army had 20,000 men led by [[Takeda Katsuyori]] and seized the opposite bank of the Tama River. However, the true purpose of the attack was feint in an attempt to draw off Hōjō forces from Shingen's real target, which was Odawara Castle. Although Shingen managed to encircle and threaten Odawara for a week, he eventually gave up and returned to Kai Province, and the forces at Takiyama Castle were also withdrawn.<ref name="Nihon"/> One result of the battle was that it revealed to Hōjō Ujiteru critical weaknesses of Takiyama Castle. Not only were the defenses of the castle obsolete due to advances in military technology (such as the introduction of gunpowder), but the site was limited geographically and the castle area could not be expanded to allow for more defense in depth. Furthermore, the location of the castle proved that it was not an obstruction for Takeda army to attack the Hōjō home territory at Odawara. Therefore, a few years after the battle, Hōjō Ujiteru relocated to {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, and by 1584 Takiyama Castle was abandoned.<ref name="Yomigaeru"/>
==Current== The castle is now only ruins, just some remnants of moats and earthworks. A bridge between Honmaru base and Nakanomaru base was reconstructed.<ref name="Kojo"/> The castle was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.<ref>{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}}</ref> It is located approximately ten minutes from "Takiyama Jyoshi Shita" bus stop of Nishi-Tokyo bus from the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hachiōji Station|en|Hachiōji Station|WD=Q800868|lt=Hachiōji Station|1=Hachiōji Station}}.

| battles = | past_commanders = {{ill|Ōishi Sadahisa|en|Ōishi Sadahisa|WD=Q11438189|lt=Ōishi Sadahisa|1=Ōishi Sadahisa}}, {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}} | garrison = | occupants = | events = Battle of Takiyama |coordinates ={{coord|35|42|7.06|N|139|19|41.85|E|region:JP-13|display=inline,title}} |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takiyama Castle'''|滝山城|Takiyama-jō}} was a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in what is now the Tani neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Hachiōji, Tokyo|Hachiōji, Tokyo]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.city.hachioji.tokyo.jp/kankobunka/002/005/p003412.html |title=滝山城|publisher=Hachiōji city official| language=Japanese | accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Its ruins have protected as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2007.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/138237|title=滝山城跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 August 2020}}</ref>
==Situation== Takiyama Castle is located on a long ridge south of the {{ill|Tama River|en|Tama River|WD=Q1063006|lt=Tama River|1=Tama River}}, next to the confluence of the Tama River coming from the [[:en:Ome, Tokyo|Ome]] area with the {{ill|Aki River|en|Aki River|WD=Q23043420|lt=Aki River|1=Aki River}} from the {{ill|Okutama, Tokyo|en|Okutama, Tokyo|WD=Q598626|lt=Okutama|1=Okutama, Tokyo}} areas of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}. The castle site and surrounding area are within the borders of the {{ill|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|en|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|WD=Q7678414|lt=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|1=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park}}.
==History== During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, this portion of Musashi Province was ruled by the Ōishi clan, vassals of the Ogigayatsu-branch of the [[Uesugi clan]] and ''{{ill|Shugodai|en|Shugodai|WD=Q568360|lt=Shugodai|1=Shugodai}}'' of Musashi Province. They were one of the major military powers in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}. Ōishi Sadashige constructed Takiyama Castle in 1521 to replace an earlier stronghold at Takatsuki, approximately one kilometer away.<ref name="Kojo">{{cite web|url=https://www.travel.co.jp/guide/article/34505/ |title=滝山城|publisher=line Travel|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> However, the Uesugi clan was soon eclipsed by the rising power of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} based at {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara|1=Odawara Castle}}, who had conquered all of neighboring {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} by around 1530. The Hōjō then set their sights north, decisively defeating the Uesugi at the 1546 {{ill|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|en|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1064720|lt=Siege of Kawagoe Castle|1=Siege of Kawagoe Castle}}. The Ōishi clan was forced to submit to {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}}, who made Takiyama Castle his field headquarters for the conquest of Musashi.
Ujiteru expanded and improved the defences of the castle.<ref name="Nihon">{{cite web|url=https://www.jp-history.info/castle/558.html |title=滝山城|date=10 May 2016 |publisher=日本の歴史ガイド|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref><ref name="Yomigaeru">{{cite web|url= http://takiyamajo.com/html/haikei.php |title=滝山城|publisher=よみがえる滝山城|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Prior to Hōjō Ujiteru, Takiyama Castle was a simple structure with two {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=enclosures|1=Kuruwa}}, the Honmaru (central bailey) or Nakanomaru (intermediate bailey) with rudimentary defenses. Ujiteru added a Ninomaru (second bailey), Sannomaru (third bailey) and Komiya Kuruwa (Komiya enclosure), each surrounded by dry moats and earthen ramparts. Each gate was fortified with a {{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=''yagura''|1=Yagura (tower)}} tower and with an [[kuruwa#umadashi|''umadashi'']] buffer area in front of the gate to make a direct front assault impossible.
In 1569, {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen|1=Takeda Shingen}}, the warlord of {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} attempted to seize the castle during the Siege of Todoriko. The Takeda army had 20,000 men led by [[Takeda Katsuyori]] and seized the opposite bank of the Tama River. However, the true purpose of the attack was feint in an attempt to draw off Hōjō forces from Shingen's real target, which was Odawara Castle. Although Shingen managed to encircle and threaten Odawara for a week, he eventually gave up and returned to Kai Province, and the forces at Takiyama Castle were also withdrawn.<ref name="Nihon"/> One result of the battle was that it revealed to Hōjō Ujiteru critical weaknesses of Takiyama Castle. Not only were the defenses of the castle obsolete due to advances in military technology (such as the introduction of gunpowder), but the site was limited geographically and the castle area could not be expanded to allow for more defense in depth. Furthermore, the location of the castle proved that it was not an obstruction for Takeda army to attack the Hōjō home territory at Odawara. Therefore, a few years after the battle, Hōjō Ujiteru relocated to {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, and by 1584 Takiyama Castle was abandoned.<ref name="Yomigaeru"/>
==Current== The castle is now only ruins, just some remnants of moats and earthworks. A bridge between Honmaru base and Nakanomaru base was reconstructed.<ref name="Kojo"/> The castle was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.<ref>{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}}</ref> It is located approximately ten minutes from "Takiyama Jyoshi Shita" bus stop of Nishi-Tokyo bus from the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hachiōji Station|en|Hachiōji Station|WD=Q800868|lt=Hachiōji Station|1=Hachiōji Station}}.
==Gallery== <gallery> 滝山城の空堀.jpg|Dry Moat of Takiyama Castle View from Nakanomaru Compound.jpg|View from Nakanomaru Compound Gate of Honmaru.jpg|Masugata gate of Honmaru Compound Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle Moat of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Dry moat of Komiya Compound </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)]]
== Literature == *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles 1540-1640 | url=https://archive.org/details/ospreyfortressja00ospr | url-access=limited | last=Turnbull| first=Stephen| year=2003| publisher=Osprey Publishing| isbn=1-84176-429-9 | page= [https://archive.org/details/ospreyfortressja00ospr/page/n62 64] pages}}
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}}]]

| battles = | past_commanders = {{ill|Ōishi Sadahisa|en|Ōishi Sadahisa|WD=Q11438189|lt=Ōishi Sadahisa|1=Ōishi Sadahisa}}, {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}} | garrison = | occupants = | events = Battle of Takiyama |coordinates ={{coord|35|42|7.06|N|139|19|41.85|E|region:JP-13|display=inline,title}} |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takiyama Castle'''|滝山城|Takiyama-jō}} was a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in what is now the Tani neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Hachiōji, Tokyo|Hachiōji, Tokyo]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.city.hachioji.tokyo.jp/kankobunka/002/005/p003412.html |title=滝山城|publisher=Hachiōji city official| language=Japanese | accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Its ruins have protected as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2007.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/138237|title=滝山城跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 August 2020}}</ref>
==Situation== Takiyama Castle is located on a long ridge south of the {{ill|Tama River|en|Tama River|WD=Q1063006|lt=Tama River|1=Tama River}}, next to the confluence of the Tama River coming from the [[:en:Ome, Tokyo|Ome]] area with the {{ill|Aki River|en|Aki River|WD=Q23043420|lt=Aki River|1=Aki River}} from the {{ill|Okutama, Tokyo|en|Okutama, Tokyo|WD=Q598626|lt=Okutama|1=Okutama, Tokyo}} areas of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}. The castle site and surrounding area are within the borders of the {{ill|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|en|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|WD=Q7678414|lt=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|1=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park}}.
==History== During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, this portion of Musashi Province was ruled by the Ōishi clan, vassals of the Ogigayatsu-branch of the [[Uesugi clan]] and ''{{ill|Shugodai|en|Shugodai|WD=Q568360|lt=Shugodai|1=Shugodai}}'' of Musashi Province. They were one of the major military powers in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}. The Hōjō then set their sights north, decisively defeating the Uesugi at the 1546 {{ill|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|en|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1064720|lt=Siege of Kawagoe Castle|1=Siege of Kawagoe Castle}}. The Ōishi clan was forced to submit to {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}}, who made Takiyama Castle his field headquarters for the conquest of Musashi.
Ujiteru expanded and improved the defences of the castle.<ref name="Nihon">{{cite web|url=https://www.jp-history.info/castle/558.html |title=滝山城|date=10 May 2016 |publisher=日本の歴史ガイド|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref><ref name="Yomigaeru">{{cite web|url= http://takiyamajo.com/html/haikei.php |title=滝山城|publisher=よみがえる滝山城|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Prior to Hōjō Ujiteru, Takiyama Castle was a simple structure with two {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=enclosures|1=Kuruwa}}, the Honmaru (central bailey) or Nakanomaru (intermediate bailey) with rudimentary defenses. Ujiteru added a Ninomaru (second bailey), Sannomaru (third bailey) and Komiya Kuruwa (Komiya enclosure), each surrounded by dry moats and earthen ramparts. Each gate was fortified with a {{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=''yagura''|1=Yagura (tower)}} tower and with an [[kuruwa#umadashi|''umadashi'']] buffer area in front of the gate to make a direct front assault impossible.
In 1569, {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen|1=Takeda Shingen}}, the warlord of {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} attempted to seize the castle during the Siege of Todoriko. Not only were the defenses of the castle obsolete due to advances in military technology (such as the introduction of gunpowder), but the site was limited geographically and the castle area could not be expanded to allow for more defense in depth. Furthermore, the location of the castle proved that it was not an obstruction for Takeda army to attack the Hōjō home territory at Odawara. Therefore, a few years after the battle, Hōjō Ujiteru relocated to {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, and by 1584 Takiyama Castle was abandoned.<ref name="Yomigaeru"/>

They were one of the major military powers in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}. Ōishi Sadashige constructed Takiyama Castle in 1521 to replace an earlier stronghold at Takatsuki, approximately one kilometer away.<ref name="Kojo">{{cite web|url=https://www.travel.co.jp/guide/article/34505/ |title=滝山城|publisher=line Travel|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> However, the Uesugi clan was soon eclipsed by the rising power of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} based at {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara|1=Odawara Castle}}, who had conquered all of neighboring {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} by around 1530. The Hōjō then set their sights north, decisively defeating the Uesugi at the 1546 {{ill|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|en|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1064720|lt=Siege of Kawagoe Castle|1=Siege of Kawagoe Castle}}. The Ōishi clan was forced to submit to {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}}, who made Takiyama Castle his field headquarters for the conquest of Musashi.
Ujiteru expanded and improved the defences of the castle.<ref name="Nihon">{{cite web|url=https://www.jp-history.info/castle/558.html |title=滝山城|date=10 May 2016 |publisher=日本の歴史ガイド|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref><ref name="Yomigaeru">{{cite web|url= http://takiyamajo.com/html/haikei.php |title=滝山城|publisher=よみがえる滝山城|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Prior to Hōjō Ujiteru, Takiyama Castle was a simple structure with two {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=enclosures|1=Kuruwa}}, the Honmaru (central bailey) or Nakanomaru (intermediate bailey) with rudimentary defenses. Ujiteru added a Ninomaru (second bailey), Sannomaru (third bailey) and Komiya Kuruwa (Komiya enclosure), each surrounded by dry moats and earthen ramparts. Each gate was fortified with a {{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=''yagura''|1=Yagura (tower)}} tower and with an [[kuruwa#umadashi|''umadashi'']] buffer area in front of the gate to make a direct front assault impossible.
In 1569, {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen|1=Takeda Shingen}}, the warlord of {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} attempted to seize the castle during the Siege of Todoriko. The Takeda army had 20,000 men led by [[Takeda Katsuyori]] and seized the opposite bank of the Tama River. However, the true purpose of the attack was feint in an attempt to draw off Hōjō forces from Shingen's real target, which was Odawara Castle. Although Shingen managed to encircle and threaten Odawara for a week, he eventually gave up and returned to Kai Province, and the forces at Takiyama Castle were also withdrawn.<ref name="Nihon"/> One result of the battle was that it revealed to Hōjō Ujiteru critical weaknesses of Takiyama Castle. Not only were the defenses of the castle obsolete due to advances in military technology (such as the introduction of gunpowder), but the site was limited geographically and the castle area could not be expanded to allow for more defense in depth. Furthermore, the location of the castle proved that it was not an obstruction for Takeda army to attack the Hōjō home territory at Odawara. Therefore, a few years after the battle, Hōjō Ujiteru relocated to {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, and by 1584 Takiyama Castle was abandoned.<ref name="Yomigaeru"/>
==Current== The castle is now only ruins, just some remnants of moats and earthworks. A bridge between Honmaru base and Nakanomaru base was reconstructed.<ref name="Kojo"/> The castle was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.<ref>{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}}</ref> It is located approximately ten minutes from "Takiyama Jyoshi Shita" bus stop of Nishi-Tokyo bus from the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hachiōji Station|en|Hachiōji Station|WD=Q800868|lt=Hachiōji Station|1=Hachiōji Station}}.
==Gallery== <gallery> 滝山城の空堀.jpg|Dry Moat of Takiyama Castle View from Nakanomaru Compound.jpg|View from Nakanomaru Compound Gate of Honmaru.jpg|Masugata gate of Honmaru Compound Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle Moat of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Dry moat of Komiya Compound </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)]]
== Literature == *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles 1540-1640 | url=https://archive.org/details/ospreyfortressja00ospr | url-access=limited | last=Turnbull| first=Stephen| year=2003| publisher=Osprey Publishing| isbn=1-84176-429-9 | page= [https://archive.org/details/ospreyfortressja00ospr/page/n62 64] pages}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[en:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[fa:قلعه تاکی‌یاما (توکیو)]][[ja:滝山城]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[en:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[fa:قلعه تاکی‌یاما (توکیو)]][[ja:滝山城]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Takiyama Castle (Tokyo)]][[fa:قلعه تاکی‌یاما (توکیو)]][[ja:滝山城]]
{{short description|Castle ruins in Hachiōji, Japan}} {{Infobox military structure | name = Takiyama Castle | nativename-a={{lang|ja|滝山城}} | partof = | location = {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji|1=Hachiōji}}, [[:en:Tokyo Prefecture|Tokyo Prefecture]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | image =Bridge of Takiyama Castle.jpg | image_size = | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Takiyama Castle | caption =Reconstructed bridge between Honmaru and Nakanomaru | type = {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Hirayama-style castle|1=Japanese castle}} | height = | ownership = Ōishi clan, {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} | controlledby = | condition = ruins | built = 1521 | builder = Ōishi clan | used = | materials = | demolished = 1576? | battles = | past_commanders = {{ill|Ōishi Sadahisa|en|Ōishi Sadahisa|WD=Q11438189|lt=Ōishi Sadahisa|1=Ōishi Sadahisa}}, {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}} | garrison = | occupants = | events = Battle of Takiyama |coordinates ={{coord|35|42|7.06|N|139|19|41.85|E|region:JP-13|display=inline,title}} |footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takiyama Castle'''|滝山城|Takiyama-jō}} was a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in what is now the Tani neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Hachiōji, Tokyo|Hachiōji, Tokyo]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.city.hachioji.tokyo.jp/kankobunka/002/005/p003412.html |title=滝山城|publisher=Hachiōji city official| language=Japanese | accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Its ruins have protected as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2007.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/138237|title=滝山城跡 |work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 August 2020}}</ref>
==Situation== Takiyama Castle is located on a long ridge south of the {{ill|Tama River|en|Tama River|WD=Q1063006|lt=Tama River|1=Tama River}}, next to the confluence of the Tama River coming from the [[:en:Ome, Tokyo|Ome]] area with the {{ill|Aki River|en|Aki River|WD=Q23043420|lt=Aki River|1=Aki River}} from the {{ill|Okutama, Tokyo|en|Okutama, Tokyo|WD=Q598626|lt=Okutama|1=Okutama, Tokyo}} areas of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}. The castle site and surrounding area are within the borders of the {{ill|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|en|Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|WD=Q7678414|lt=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park|1=Takiyama Prefectural Natural Park}}.
==History== During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, this portion of Musashi Province was ruled by the Ōishi clan, vassals of the Ogigayatsu-branch of the [[Uesugi clan]] and ''{{ill|Shugodai|en|Shugodai|WD=Q568360|lt=Shugodai|1=Shugodai}}'' of Musashi Province. They were one of the major military powers in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}. Ōishi Sadashige constructed Takiyama Castle in 1521 to replace an earlier stronghold at Takatsuki, approximately one kilometer away.<ref name="Kojo">{{cite web|url=https://www.travel.co.jp/guide/article/34505/ |title=滝山城|publisher=line Travel|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> However, the Uesugi clan was soon eclipsed by the rising power of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} based at {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara|1=Odawara Castle}}, who had conquered all of neighboring {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} by around 1530. The Hōjō then set their sights north, decisively defeating the Uesugi at the 1546 {{ill|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|en|Siege of Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1064720|lt=Siege of Kawagoe Castle|1=Siege of Kawagoe Castle}}. The Ōishi clan was forced to submit to {{ill|Hōjō Ujiteru|en|Hōjō Ujiteru|WD=Q2790462|lt=Hōjō Ujiteru|1=Hōjō Ujiteru}}, who made Takiyama Castle his field headquarters for the conquest of Musashi.
Ujiteru expanded and improved the defences of the castle.<ref name="Nihon">{{cite web|url=https://www.jp-history.info/castle/558.html |title=滝山城|date=10 May 2016 |publisher=日本の歴史ガイド|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref><ref name="Yomigaeru">{{cite web|url= http://takiyamajo.com/html/haikei.php |title=滝山城|publisher=よみがえる滝山城|language=Japanese|accessdate=9 August 2019}}</ref> Prior to Hōjō Ujiteru, Takiyama Castle was a simple structure with two {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=enclosures|1=Kuruwa}}, the Honmaru (central bailey) or Nakanomaru (intermediate bailey) with rudimentary defenses. Ujiteru added a Ninomaru (second bailey), Sannomaru (third bailey) and Komiya Kuruwa (Komiya enclosure), each surrounded by dry moats and earthen ramparts. Each gate was fortified with a {{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=''yagura''|1=Yagura (tower)}} tower and with an [[kuruwa#umadashi|''umadashi'']] buffer area in front of the gate to make a direct front assault impossible.
In 1569, {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen|1=Takeda Shingen}}, the warlord of {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} attempted to seize the castle during the Siege of Todoriko. The Takeda army had 20,000 men led by [[Takeda Katsuyori]] and seized the opposite bank of the Tama River. However, the true purpose of the attack was feint in an attempt to draw off Hōjō forces from Shingen's real target, which was Odawara Castle. Although Shingen managed to encircle and threaten Odawara for a week, he eventually gave up and returned to Kai Province, and the forces at Takiyama Castle were also withdrawn.<ref name="Nihon"/> One result of the battle was that it revealed to Hōjō Ujiteru critical weaknesses of Takiyama Castle. Not only were the defenses of the castle obsolete due to advances in military technology (such as the introduction of gunpowder), but the site was limited geographically and the castle area could not be expanded to allow for more defense in depth. Furthermore, the location of the castle proved that it was not an obstruction for Takeda army to attack the Hōjō home territory at Odawara. Therefore, a few years after the battle, Hōjō Ujiteru relocated to {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, and by 1584 Takiyama Castle was abandoned.<ref name="Yomigaeru"/>
==Current== The castle is now only ruins, just some remnants of moats and earthworks. A bridge between Honmaru base and Nakanomaru base was reconstructed.<ref name="Kojo"/> The castle was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.<ref>{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 July 2019}}</ref> It is located approximately ten minutes from "Takiyama Jyoshi Shita" bus stop of Nishi-Tokyo bus from the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hachiōji Station|en|Hachiōji Station|WD=Q800868|lt=Hachiōji Station|1=Hachiōji Station}}.
==Gallery== <gallery> 滝山城の空堀.jpg|Dry Moat of Takiyama Castle View from Nakanomaru Compound.jpg|View from Nakanomaru Compound Gate of Honmaru.jpg|Masugata gate of Honmaru Compound Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Honmaru Compound of Takiyama Castle Moat of Takiyama Castle.jpg|Dry moat of Komiya Compound </gallery>
